questions.question: Explain the difference between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. Give one advantage of Harvard architecture.
keyPoints: Von Neumann uses single memory for instructions and data with shared bus
Harvard uses separate memories for instructions and data with separate buses
Advantage: Simultaneous instruction fetch and data access increases throughput
Harvard eliminates bottleneck of fetching instructions and data on same bus

questions.question: Describe the basic instruction cycle with a neat diagram.
keyPoints: Fetch: Get instruction from memory using address in PC
Decode: Interpret the instruction format and operands
Execute: Perform operation specified by instruction
Store: Write result back to memory or register
PC is updated after each instruction fetch

questions.question: What is DMA? How does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: DMA stands for Direct Memory Access
Allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention
CPU is freed to perform other tasks during transfer
Reduces CPU overhead for I/O operations
Commonly used for disk, network, and audio/video transfers

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the Memory Management Unit (MMU).
keyPoints: MMU translates virtual addresses to physical addresses
Enables virtual memory functionality
Provides memory protection between processes
Handles page table management
Allows processes to use more memory than physically available

questions.question: What are interrupts? Explain the types of interrupts.
keyPoints: Interrupt is a signal requiring immediate attention
Hardware Interrupt: Generated by I/O devices, timer, hardware failures
Software Interrupt: Generated by programs (system calls)
Types: Maskable (can be ignored), Non-maskable (cannot be ignored)
Interrupt handling involves saving state, servicing interrupt, resuming execution

questions.question: Explain the different types of buses in a computer system.
keyPoints: Data Bus: Carries actual data between components (bidirectional)
Address Bus: Carries memory addresses (unidirectional, CPU to memory)
Control Bus: Carries control signals like read/write, interrupt, clock
System bus combines all three for integrated communication
Bus width affects data transfer speed (more lines = more data)

questions.question: Describe the memory hierarchy of a computer system.
keyPoints: Level 1: Registers - fastest, smallest, inside CPU
Level 2: Cache - fast, small, between CPU and RAM
Level 3: Main Memory (RAM) - moderate speed and size
Level 4: Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD) - slow, large, non-volatile
Trade-off between speed, cost, and capacity

questions.question: Explain the organization of a computer system with a block diagram. Discuss the functions of CPU, memory, and I/O devices.
keyPoints: Block diagram showing CPU connected to memory and I/O via system bus
CPU functions: Executes instructions, controls operations, processes data
Memory functions: Stores instructions and data temporarily (RAM) or permanently (ROM)
I/O functions: Interface for input devices (keyboard, mouse) and output devices (monitor, printer)
System bus connects all components for data, address, and control signal transfer
Role of ALU and Control Unit within CPU explained

questions.question: Explain the concept of interrupts in computer systems. Discuss the interrupt handling mechanism and its advantages over polling.
keyPoints: Interrupt: Signal to CPU indicating event requiring attention
Types: Hardware (external devices), Software (program requests), Timer interrupts
Handling: CPU saves current state, identifies interrupt source, executes ISR, restores state
Advantages over polling: CPU not wasted checking device status, faster response, efficient for rare events
Polling disadvantages: Wastes CPU cycles, higher latency, less efficient

questions.question: What is the difference between RISC and CISC architectures? Discuss the advantages of RISC processors.
keyPoints: RISC: Reduced Instruction Set Computer - simple, few instructions, fixed size
CISC: Complex Instruction Set Computer - many instructions, variable length, microcode
RISC advantages: Simpler design, faster execution per instruction, more parallel execution
RISC advantages: Lower power consumption, easier to pipeline, lower cost
CISC advantages: Fewer instructions in program, more complex operations at hardware level
Modern processors often use hybrid approach combining both philosophies

questions.question: Describe the internal organization of the CPU with a neat diagram. Explain the functions of ALU, Control Unit, and Registers.
keyPoints: CPU consists of ALU, Control Unit, and Registers connected by internal bus
ALU: Performs arithmetic operations (add, sub, mul, div) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, compare)
Control Unit: Fetches instructions, decodes them, generates control signals for execution
Registers: Fastest memory - PC (next instruction), IR (current instruction), MAR (memory address), MBR (data buffer), ACC (accumulator)
Internal data path connects these components for data flow during execution
